narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict their chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it flagged a high risk for you. The model estimated an 85.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which directly led to the rejection of your admission because the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and pass that critical hurdle. The biggest factor pushing this prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom 10% of your peers that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile. This low performance carried the strongest weight in the model's decision, as it strongly signals challenges in sustaining the academic rigor needed for law school and the bar. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 22, which is significantly lower than the average of about 37 across past students, further tilting the prediction toward failure by highlighting potential gaps in test-taking skills essential for legal exams. Rounding out the top concerns was your Year 1 decile also at 1, again well below the average, reinforcing a pattern of early underperformance that the model saw as a continued risk. Other aspects, like your middle family income bracket or planning to attend full-time like most students, played lesser roles and didn't offset these core academic red flags. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have stronger decile rankings and higher LSATs, your profile stood out as riskier for bar passage despite your full-time commitment. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many factors like personal circumstances can influence grades—but the model weighs these metrics heavily based on historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking targeted academic advising to improve your decile rankings in future evaluations. Reapplying after addressing these could shift the model's view in your favor, and many students in similar situations have succeeded by focusing on these steps. You're taking a thoughtful path toward law, and with targeted improvements, stronger opportunities await.
